---
title: "Installing Windsurf and Getting Started with Agentic Coding"
subtitle: "DSHB Professional Development Conference — June 24, 2026"
---

::: {.session-meta}
Session 1 · June 24, 2026 · 2:00 – 3:10 pm
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Step One: Read, Re-read, and Read Again

Before you do anything else, read the starter instructions on this page in
their entirety. Then read them again. Then a third time. The single most
common failure mode for groups in this assignment is skimming the starter
material once, getting stuck at minute 20, and then having to backtrack.
Twenty minutes of careful reading at the start saves an hour of frustration
later.
:::

## What You're Teaching

Windsurf is an agentic IDE built on top of VS Code. "Agentic" means the
editor can take multi-step actions on your behalf — reading files, running
commands, editing code across a project — based on natural-language
instructions, and then return the result for you to review. Your peers will
leave this session able to install Windsurf, configure it for a real project,
and run their first agentic task.

## Mini Quick-Start

Your group's job is to walk through this entire flow yourselves before you
teach it. Plan on at least one multi-hour solo session per group member, plus
a group rehearsal.

### 1. Install Windsurf

Download Windsurf from the official site and install it on your operating
system. Pay attention to the system requirements — older machines may need
the lighter-weight build.

- Official site: <https://windsurf.com/>
- Confirm your install by opening Windsurf, signing in, and creating an empty
  project.

### 2. Open a Real Project

Clone a small public repository (one of your own from earlier coursework
works well) and open it in Windsurf. The agentic features feel abstract on a
blank project — they shine when there's actual code to reason about.

### 3. Run Your First Agentic Task

Try one of these as your first prompt:

- "Read the README and tell me what this project does."
- "Add a docstring to every function in `analysis.R` (or `analysis.py`)."
- "Find any places where I'm hard-coding file paths and refactor them to use
  a config variable."

Watch what the agent proposes *before* accepting any changes. The ability to
inspect, accept, or reject diffs is the core skill.

### 4. Tour the Three Modes

Windsurf has more than one way to invoke the agent (chat, in-line edits,
multi-file actions). Get comfortable switching between them. Your demo should
show at least two.

## Suggested Lecture Notes

(Note: This subsection created as a demonstration)

- **Define agentic coding clearly.** An agentic IDE does not just autocomplete the next few tokens. It plans multi-step actions, calls tools to read files and run commands, edits across a project, and surfaces the result as a reviewable diff. The unit of work is a task, not a keystroke.
- **Contrast with traditional autocomplete.** Copilot-style autocomplete predicts what comes next at the cursor. An agent decides which files to open, what to change, what to test, and then asks you to approve. Make sure peers leave understanding that this is a category difference, not a feature upgrade.
- **Place Windsurf in the broader landscape.** Windsurf is built on the open-source VS Code core, so the editor UX, keybindings, and extension marketplace will feel familiar to anyone who has used VS Code. Cascade (the embedded agent) is the differentiator. Other tools in this category include Cursor, Cline, and the Claude Code CLI.
- **Walk through the agent loop in plain English.** Read context → propose a plan → call tools (search, edit, run a command) → present a diff → human accepts, rejects, or revises. Every step is interruptible. Drawing this loop on the board once is worth ten minutes of demo.
- **"Human in the loop" is the work, not a formality.** Agents hallucinate file paths, invent function names, and over-edit. Reviewing the diff *is* the engineering. If your peers walk away clicking "accept all" on every proposal, the lesson has failed.
- **Scope the context.** Agents work better on small, well-scoped project folders than on sprawling monorepos. Opening a focused project beats dragging in your entire `~/Projects` directory. Encourage peers to start from a clean folder for their first attempts.
- **Prompt specificity beats prompt cleverness.** "Clean this up" produces sprawling unreviewable diffs. "Rename `df1` to `responses` in `analysis.py` and update all references" produces a diff a human can actually evaluate. Specific in, specific out.
- **Cost and latency are real.** Each agent turn calls a frontier model. Long unattended runs are expensive and hard to audit after the fact. Build the habit of checking the agent's work between turns rather than chaining ten requests and walking away.
- **Privacy and data handling.** Anything in your open files may be sent to a model provider. Do not paste credentials, API keys, PHI, or unredacted human-subjects data into the editor. For a behavioral data science program this matters more than usual. Flag it explicitly.
- **Reproducibility tradeoff.** Agentic edits are stochastic. The same prompt run twice can produce different diffs. Commit early, commit often, and treat the agent's output like a pull request from a collaborator you trust but verify.
- **Three invocation surfaces, three blast radii.** The chat panel is for conversation and exploration. Inline edit is for surgical changes at the cursor. Full agent mode is for cross-project, multi-file tasks. Match the surface to the size of the task. Do not reach for the biggest hammer by default.
- **Memories and rules earn their keep.** Windsurf supports per-project rules and persistent memories. Use them to encode conventions like "snake_case for variables" or "tests live in `tests/`" so the agent stops asking and stops drifting. This is one of the highest-leverage features and the most often skipped.
- **Watch what tools the agent invokes.** When it proposes a shell command, read it before approving. Pay special attention to anything destructive (`rm`, `git reset --hard`, `pip install`, anything that touches a database). The convenience of "just approve" is exactly how accidents happen.
- **Git is the safety net.** Branch before a big agentic task. Let the agent work. Review the diff. Commit or `git restore` accordingly. Do not run a thirty-step agent loop directly on `main`. This habit alone prevents most of the "the AI broke my project" stories.
- **The transferable skill is reading diffs critically.** Tools come and go. Windsurf may not be the dominant agentic IDE in two years. The discipline of recognizing when the agent is wrong, pushing back, and accepting only the parts that are correct is the skill that survives the next tool change. Frame the whole session around that point.

## Suggested Resources

- **Official docs:** <https://docs.windsurf.com/>
- **Quick-start tutorial:** linked from the Windsurf homepage; the
  "First-time user" walkthrough is the best place to start.
- **Anthropic / Claude resources:** Windsurf integrates with Claude models;
  some peers may already have API keys from earlier in the program.
- **Windsurf community:** <https://windsurf.com/community>
- **Windsurf blog:** <https://windsurf.com/blog>

{{< include _outline-template.qmd >}}

## Topic-Specific Demo Tips

- **As you set Windsurf up for yourselves, keep track of common mistakes, errors, misunderstandings, and points of friction.** Designate a member of your team to centrally collect sign-up issues, model-selection confusion, prompts that didn't go where peers expected, and any IDE-specific quirks across Mac and Windows. Then write them up into a one-page "troubleshooting guide" you can share with the others in class on the day of via the course chat platforms.
- **Use a "team teaching" approach.** To make this lesson successful, every single member of your team will need to be confident installing Windsurf, signing in, opening a project, writing a basic prompt, reading the proposed diff, and accepting or rejecting the agent's suggestion. The members of your team will need to circulate through the room helping other students. There will be many stumbling blocks.
- **Have a fallback project ready.** If the demo project has issues on the
  classroom Wi-Fi, you want a small local project you can pivot to without
  losing momentum.
- **Pre-stage your prompts.** Live-typing prompts during a demo invites
  typos and dead air. Write your three or four demo prompts in advance and
  paste them in.
- **Show a rejection.** Don't just show prompts that work. Show one where
  the agent proposes something wrong, and demonstrate how you push back. This
  is one of the most valuable things peers can learn.
